event: Early Stone Age (Acheulean) tool-making hominins inhabit the region of present-day Gabon, leaving some of Central Africa's oldest archaeological traces.
Hunter-gatherer communities, ancestors of the Babongo and other Pygmy peoples, occupy the dense equatorial rainforests of the area.
Bantu-speaking peoples begin migrating into the region, gradually introducing agriculture and settled village life.
Iron-working spreads through the territory as Bantu migration intensifies, transforming forest agriculture and toolmaking.
Successive waves of Bantu clans, including Myene-speaking and Fang peoples, establish kinship-based communities across the coast and interior.
Portuguese navigators reach the Gabon estuary, naming it 'Gabão' (cloak) for the shape of the Komo River mouth.
Portuguese and other European traders begin coastal commerce in ivory, hardwoods, and increasingly enslaved people along the estuary.
The Orungu people establish a coastal kingdom around Cape Lopez that grows wealthy as a middleman in the Atlantic slave trade.
The Orungu kingdom reaches its commercial peak, controlling much of the slave and ivory trade through the port at Cape Lopez.
French naval officer Édouard Bouët-Willaumez signs a treaty with local Mpongwe ruler King Denis, securing France's first foothold on the estuary.
American Protestant missionaries establish a mission near the estuary, among the earliest in the region.
Freed slaves from a captured slave ship are settled at a new site named Libreville ('Free Town'), the future capital.
France formally annexes coastal territory, consolidating control over the Gabon estuary.
Explorer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza begins expeditions up the Ogooué River, extending French claims into the interior.
The Berlin Conference's partition of Africa recognizes French sovereignty over Gabon during the Scramble for Africa.
Gabon is established as a distinct French colony separate from the wider Congo administration.
Gabon becomes one of the four territories of the newly created Federation of French Equatorial Africa, governed from Brazzaville.
Physician Albert Schweitzer founds his renowned hospital at Lambaréné on the Ogooué River.
Free French forces, after the Battle of Gabon, wrest the colony from Vichy control during World War II.
Gabon becomes a French overseas territory with its own representation in the French Parliament.
France's loi-cadre grants Gabon a territorial assembly and greater internal self-government.
Gabon votes to become an autonomous republic within the French Community.
Gabon gains full independence from France on 17 August, with Léon M'ba as its first president.
Gabon joins the United Nations and adopts a new constitution establishing a presidential system.
A coup briefly ousts President Léon M'ba, but French military intervention swiftly restores him to power.
President M'ba dies and is succeeded by Vice President Omar (then Albert-Bernard) Bongo.
Bongo establishes the Gabonese Democratic Party (PDG) as the sole legal party, creating a one-party state.
President Bongo converts to Islam, taking the name Omar, as Gabon expands its oil-fueled prosperity.
Gabon joins OPEC, reflecting its growing importance as a Central African oil producer.
The completion of the Trans-Gabon Railway links the interior to the coast, boosting timber and mineral exports.
Pro-democracy protests and a national conference force Bongo to legalize multiparty politics, ending one-party rule.
Gabon holds its first multiparty presidential election, which Omar Bongo wins amid opposition allegations of fraud.
Gabon withdraws from OPEC as its oil output declines.
President Bongo establishes 13 national parks, dedicating about 11 percent of the country to conservation.
Omar Bongo dies after 41 years in power, and his son Ali Bongo Ondimba wins the presidential election to succeed him.
Gabon co-hosts the Africa Cup of Nations, showcasing major stadium investments.
Ali Bongo is declared the winner of a disputed reelection, sparking deadly protests and the burning of parliament.
Gabon hosts the Africa Cup of Nations as sole host nation.
A short-lived military coup attempt is staged in Libreville while President Bongo recovers abroad from a stroke.
Military officers led by General Brice Oligui Nguema seize power, ending the Bongo family's 55-year rule after another disputed election.
Gabonese voters approve a new constitution in a referendum, laying the groundwork for a return to civilian rule.
Brice Oligui Nguema wins a presidential election, formalizing his leadership in the post-Bongo era.
